Structure: Abilene Christian University manages KACU, the public radio station that serves the greater Abilene area and directly engages student reporters, editors and producers to work alongside professionals in the newsroom.
The program is connected with 3 media organizations (BCTV, WEEU AM, Spotlight PA Burks Edition) and students helped fill a gap in arts & culture reporting.

Cronkite Sports class, a capstone class for upperclassmen majoring in journalism as well as master’s students, acts as a teaching hospital for student journalists who produce sports stories that air on Arizona PBS, its main partner, and more than 170 additional publication partners regularly publish their content.

In this capstone class, Students learn how to report and produce Spanish-language content across audio, broadcast, digital and social media, and showcase their work on Cronkite News and is distributed broadly.
Students report from D.C. and content is shared on Cronkite news channels and with media partners.

Arizona State's Cronkite News class, a capstone class for upperclassmen majoring in journalism as well as master’s students, acts as a teaching hospital for student journalists. The statehouse program is just one part of ASU’s Cronkite News class. Students take the class for credit. Students either pitch the story ideas or they are assigned story. Faculty members edit the work. Cronkite News also operates two websites that carry digital versions of its stories. One site is designated for the public to access those stories in readable format. The other is available only to partner news organizations across Arizona, which can pull content from that online menu to offer to their own readers, listeners and viewers. Partners subscribe to the daily Cronkite News digest and have unrestricted access to use Cronkite work however they choose, as long as they give the students credit. Cronkite News uses Slack with a pick-up channel that shows where its stories go. Any news organization can ask Cronkite News to join its listserv for access to the digest for free.

Program Name: Howard Center for Investigative Journalism at ASU
Structure: The Howard Center for Investigative Journalism at Arizona State University, funded by the Scripps Howard Foundation, produces national investigations in collaboration with other university journalism programs and professional partners such as CBS News, USA Today, The Associated Press, NPR and PBS NewsHour.

Arizona State's Cronkite News class, a capstone class for upperclassmen majoring in journalism as well as master’s students, acts as a teaching hospital for student journalists. The statehouse program is just one part of ASU’s Cronkite News class. Students take the class for credit. Students either pitch the story ideas or they are assigned story. Faculty members edit the work. Cronkite News also operates two websites that carry digital versions of its stories. One site is designated for the public to access those stories in readable format. The other is available only to partner news organizations across Arizona, which can pull content from that online menu to offer to their own readers, listeners and viewers. Partners subscribe to the daily Cronkite News digest and have unrestricted access to use Cronkite work however they choose, as long as they give the students credit. Cronkite News uses Slack with a pick-up channel that shows where its stories go. Any news organization can ask Cronkite News to join its listserv for access to the digest for free.

The program houses graphic design, journalism, and TV production, and is under the journalism program (The Student Newsroom) where students produce content for the local radio station. Stories produced in the newsroom are also informally shared with the local newspaper and are edited by a professional journalist who previously worked for the local newspaper. Students enroll in the program through a paid internship course and receive credit.

Lead Faculty Name: Lillie Fears/ she provided Johnathan Reeves email
Structure: The Delta Digital News Service is a practicum course that is required for students doing a news emphasis. The program is facilitated by an advisor, and through each practicum course, students report news in the Mississippi Delta in Arkansas. Students assume various newsroom roles, including reporting on specific beats (such as education, local government and health) and sometimes also take on editing roles that involve more management of the service. All news stories students produce are published on the website. Any news publication can republish the stories, giving credit to the news service and the student journalist.
Students in classes receive edits from faculty, while student editing is conducted in the student media program.

Students complete work during capstone class and focus on sustainability issues in Indiana. Work then appears in USA Today-affiliated outlets. Collaborators include the Lafayette Journal and Courier, jconline.com; Muncie Star Press, thestarpress.com; Richmond Palladium-Item, pal-item.com; also Indiana University–Purdue University Indianapolis and Indiana University's Environmental Resilience Institute. Student stories typically have at least 2,000 page views. Students work first with instructor Adam Kuban, then send stories to USA Today regional editor Deanna Watson for review and publication. Students' reporting is completed through a capstone course taught by Kuban.

Institution Name: Bloomfield College of Montclair State University
Structure: Students report for Essex News Daily, which serves as Bloomfield College's local media partner.
Students use the latest computational tools combined with robust field reporting to produce data-driven, investigative news stories for local, regional and national news organizations.

Boston University’s student reporting program is a large-scale, faculty- edited, credit-based newsroom that operates entirely through coursework rather than internships or paid positions. They can earn 4-8 credits. The newsroom is integrated into multiple credit-bearing courses, including a State House reporting initiative. In 2025, 85 students produced 511 stories, all of which were published through partnerships with 35 local news outlets in and around Boston. In 2026 the program expanded to include more than 100 students per semester. 

Students conduct all reporting, but every story undergoes full faculty editing prior to publication, and no content is distributed without faculty approval. Faculty work closely with partner outlets to identify content needs, accept assignments directly from editors, and pitch story ideas, resulting in a 100% publication rate. Partners are primarily small independent outlets and local nonprofits experiencing reporting shortages. The program prohibits the use of generative AI for writing, though students may use AI tools in a limited, research-oriented manner akin to search engines. Overall, the program exemplifies a highly structured, faculty-led model that integrates intensive editorial oversight with strong, demand-driven partnerships in local journalism.
Pilot partnership with local access television to provide student reporting on local communities.
The journalism professors who teach reporting classes partner with the town's local non-profit all-digital news outlet. Students from the classes conduct off-campus public affairs reporting.

The Reporting Lab at Brandeis is a community-focused journalism initiative which consists of several interconnected programs that provide students with opportunities to report on under-covered communities, experiment with emerging storytelling formats and build their portfolios.

Students in a broadcast operations class and a digital and broadcast production class work in partnership with the Buffalo Broadcasters Association to produce a weekly local news panel discussion program that airs on Buffalo television station WNYO. They also distribute it in a print.

Student reporters cover the neighborhood that surrounds the university and produce multimedia content for a dedicated website, Buffalo Review West; their work provides news coverage for an area that lost its community newspaper several years ago.

Cal Poly journalism department started a collaboration with the local NPR affiliate, KCBX, in which the radio station hires one "super intern" who coodinates the public meeting/community coverage. Patrick helps identify this intern. The other students help produce the content and get some compensation, but the main intern plays the part of an assignment editor.

The faculty champion has a journalism program which has a latinx journalism concentration. Newswritings in Spanish course partners with their local ABC news, where students prepare a package and the partner uploads it to their Spanish website.

Program Name: Partnership with Voice of Orange County
Structure: This is a new course-based program that is starting in 2026. A former entertainment reporter was hired as adjunct to teach the entertainment course for the first time Spring 2026.
The lead faculty works with a local news partner to identify a topic to investigate and her class works on that project throughout the semester to produce a deeply-reported story package.

Program Name: Race, Class & Gender in American Journalism (Podcasting)
Structure: Students produce audio content that are offered up to local news partners. The class started in 2019-2020.

The course counts towards students' upper-division writing intensive requirement, and stories are published on the "Community News" tab on the school's University Times website, though the program is separate from the student media organization.

The university has a standalone Department of Journalism and Public Relations, which encompasses the student newspaper--The Current, DIG Magazine, and what the faculty founded & oversees, ENYE. Funding for the faculty champion's paper comes from the university. The paper covers the latinx community throughout the campus, the city, and southern California. A print magazine is published once each semester and other content is regularly posted on their website, social media, and podcast. Between 15-20 students take a course to work as reporters for the magazine and 6 other volunteers work as copy editors, translators, designers, or photographers. The program also employs 3-4 editors paid through the university's grant.

818 Beat serves as a platform where CSU Northridge student-produced community news (audio, Video, multimedia, print) can be packaged into a shareable, republishable format. Quality work from faculty-led skills courses (earlier in curriculum) are funneled to the faaculty editor and class who repackage the stories and publish them on the 818 Beat website.
Advisor selects and trains students for the program's editorial team. Editorial team has the independence to publish stories, but the advisor oversees and can strike down stories if needed (rarely).

The faculty champion teaches journalism classes and works with local outlets to publish student content. Along wiht collleagues in the Local News Partnerships Initiates, the faculty coordinate with local outlets in need of reporting.

The course, offered once a year, is required for students in the journalism concentration in the communications department. The class meets for one three-hour session each week, then students pursue reporting projects in communities in the southwestern Vermont area. The class has direct connections with four local news publications. In some cases, Editors will give specific assignments to the class, which the instructor distributes among students. Other times, students pursue their own stories through class assignments. Throughout the semester, students write several different kinds of stories (i.e., government news, community Q&As) which Blow then offers to local publications. Student work is also published on the Community News Service website, where news outlets can pick up stories and republish for free.
Story assignments in which students produce content are shared with the statewide collaborative which reshares the content for news organizations in the the state.

CIM Reporting Project features reporting on local prisons and collaborates with rising scholars that go into prison and work with incarcerated journalism students in addition to reporting on prison students.

The Collegiate News Program is a collaboration between Chapman University journalism program and local nonprofit news service Voice of OC. Orange County has 34 cities and students work to provide coverage to "news deserts" in this region. Students publish in a series of courses beginning with basic news coverage and civic reporting. Students are assigned to cover the same cities as they move from introductory reporting into upperlevel undergraduate advanced reporting coursework, finally completing a two-course senior seminar sequence, during which they report and write more complex enterprise and investigative projects.

At Clackamas Print, student journalism operates as a fully curricular, faculty-led media model embedded within 200-level news courses at a suburban community college outside Portland. Approximately 60–75 students enroll annually across two core classes, producing around 100 stories per year that are published primarily on the student media website, with one print edition released each academic term. Coverage focuses overwhelmingly on campus life due to limited staffing capacity—roughly 17 students at any given time—though occasional arts, culture, sports, and community stories are produced. Editorial oversight is centralized, with faculty editing student media work internally, while any stories placed with external outlets are edited by professional newsroom staff. Historically, students regularly pitched to local news organizations amid widespread community news closures, but opportunities have narrowed following industry consolidation, including the sale of a key regional publisher, reducing internship pathways in Clackamas County. The program has maintained a small, grant- and fundraising-supported paid internship pipeline, including placements at The Oregonian, though these internships are newsroom-directed and geographically oriented toward downtown Portland rather than the immediate suburban community. A strict no-AI policy is codified in course syllabi, reinforcing traditional reporting ethics and authorship norms within the student media environment.

Students in several journalism courses write stories for The Panther, Claflin University’s student newspaper which is distributed on- and off-campus; some faculty edited stories are also published in the local daily, The Times and Democrat.

Program Name: Clark Urban Community Journalism Class Blog
Structure: Student reported urban city stories are edited and shared with the Worcester Telegram and Gazette.

The Journalism Institute is a freestanding program at Colorado College that prioritizes hands-on reporting experience and long-form journalism projects through partnerships with local news publications and other organizations.

The Journalism and Communication program at Columbia College Chicago has a course, JOUR 265 Reporting in Spanish (Reportaje en Español) that is offered every two years. The course was created in 2002 and was offered twice in 2003. It was then taught in 2008, 2010, 2012, 2014, 2016, 2018, 2023. It did not run in 2025 due to low enrollment. I have been teaching it since 2010, when I started sending stories for publication. But it was officially from 2012 onwards that I systematically began submitting all the student-produced articles to the professional media with the goal of having them published.
Chicago Talks is housed at the college and releases stories produced in classes. Audience is external. A student editor is hired every year as a work-study who is overseen by a faculty.

Students produce most of the stories and content for AustinTalks.org, a hyperlocal news site that showcases the diverse community of the west-side Chicago neighborhood, and earn a minimum wage or course credit.

Columbia News Service publishes multimedia journalism by Columbia Journalism School students across a range of topics, including local news, and makes it available for republishing by other news organizations.
In City Newsroom, a 15-week graduate-level multimedia course, students serve as local beat reporters covering New York City and create text, video and audio stories.

Students publish their work in the online student newspaper and partners with their local NPR station, WYPR, under the direction of a faculty coordinator to produce a 3-5 minute solo-commentary on a single topic they learned over the semester. These projects are then published in WYPR's "Know It All" podcast.
Through the statewide Connecticut Student Journalism Collaborative (CTSJC), students submit stories that are shared across the state to fill the gaps in local coverage.

Institution Name: Craig Newmark CUNY Graduate School and University Center
Structure: NYCity News Service covers New York City neighborhoods and produces special news reports on a variety of topics; the service feeds stories to various news partners and makes its content available for republication.

The program intends to work with the San Luis Obispo Tribune, the main local newspaper. Students will participate in the program and earn credit through associated classses (student newspaper and news writing classes) with a focus on providing coverage primarily of the community college.

Students have conducted internship with news organizations in the New York area and the faculty advisor is seeking to build these relationships and create publication labs for a news/academic partnership.

Students work on The Full360, a student-run digital news site under faculty leadership. Students produce content for class, develop reporting skills, and engage in real-world publishing with local news outlets. Internship & freelance pathways:  Students collaborate with The Bucks County Herald to publish student photos and stories.

The Reporting Project is a community-centered journalism initiative that combines coursework, co-curricular opportunities, and paid experience to immerse students in locally-focused journalism with content shared and published in numerous platforms.

Classes and student media regularly partner with: Better Government Association (investigative nonprofit), City Bureau, Block Club Chicago, WTTW PBS Chicago, Chicago Reader, and others. More than one hundred students are involved in the partnership and their work reaches millions of viewers. Faculty teach a variety of journalism courses that allow students to create work for publication with media partners.

In this statewide collaborative, eight universities including four HBCUs are partnering together to provide community news through paid student reporting year-round: Louisiana State University and Agricultural & Mechanical College; Grambling University (HBCU), Dillard University (HBCU), Xavier University (HBCU), Southern University (HBCU), Nicholls State University, Northwestern State University and Southeastern University. Each school will have initially two to four students who will get paid $12 an hour for up to 10 hours of work a week during the fall and spring semesters. Additionally, each school will host one full-time summer internship where the student will be paid $12 an hour to write stories for their local community outlets. Reporting can include print, digital, broadcast and social media. To encourage more stories out of classrooms, students reporting for a class can earn a $100 stipend for any publishable story at any of these schools. Individual schools can decide whether students can also earn credit by participating this program.
Student reporters cover Durham and state politics and policy and content is emailed to a list of stakeholders/media partners and shared in a direct partnership with Indie Week.
Through the statewide Connecticut Student Journalism Collaborative (CTSJC), students submit stories that are shared across the state to fill the gaps in local coverage.

WEIU takes in students from Eastern Illinois University who volunteer to work at their daily live newscast at 5:30pm, which run by students and 1 staff overseer. Stories are checked by up to 3 different journalists in the School of Communications in Journalism.Students earn credits if they take an internship or practicum through the school.
In Radio and TV Newswriting students put together an audio story, either a 6-8 minute feature or a 3-5 minute Storycorps-style piece which is shared with WEMU, the local radio station.
This program has partnerships with 50 news outlets, allowing student work to be published widely.

Program Name: Professional Development in Communication and Media Studies
Structure: The university has a professional development course where students are required to work 30 hours in student media and has had a partnership with an external outlet, the Fayetteville Observer. Students who take this course must publish their stories in order to receive the credit hours.
Students report on on-campus and local news for Fitchburg State’s student newspaper, The Point and local cable access TV station and republished in other local media outlets.

Florida A&M University coordinates a statehouse capital bureau through an advanced reporting class.  The class is a required course for the journalism majors at FAMU.  The work is published on the student newspaper’s website and available to other outlets to republish.

Guided by experienced journalism faculty, students produce stories for a community news site that serves as a source of content for major local media outlets including The Miami Herald, The Palm Beach Post, The South Florida Sun-Sentinel and WLRN, an NPR affiliate.

Program Name: WGCU News/ Previously known as Democracy Watch
Structure: The reporting courses are focused on Southwest Florida government bodies. Students report on issues before those bodies, and how the outcomes of government actions affect citizens. Faculty are involved in all aspects of the production of content as well as teaching the courses associated with the program. The program is also taught in collaboration with WGCU News, therefore many of the Democracy Watch stories wind up on the WGCU News webpage, and some on air.
A faculty-advised, hands on program involving both new and traditional media including multi-media reporting with content shared with Miami news outlets.
A faculty-advised, hands on program producing content in Spanish, involving both new and traditional media including multi-media reporting with content shared with Miami news outlets.

The Statehouse File is the flagship immersive program of the Pulliam School of Journalism at Franklin College. Covering its 20th legislative session in 2025, it offers daily Indiana politics and culture coverage from a full-time office in the Indiana Statehouse. Every Franklin multimedia journalism major completes a 12-credit internship semester at the Statehouse, but students can also earn one activity credit, take a three-credit class, or work as a freelancer for a stipend under the guidance of experienced journalist and editor Colleen Steffen. TheStatehouseFile.com operates year-round with between three and 12 student journalists.
The program/partners with the Georgia Recorder which aligns with is the College to Career Initiative.

The Center for Community Media is both course-based and experiential, offering student media opportunities through a digital newspaper, an internet radio station, and a TV show. Some opportunities are tied to courses or workshops, while others allow for direct student participation. The Center partners with faculty across disciplines, such as History, English, and Political Science, to train students to report on local government meetings. There is also a retired journalist/editor supervising the student newspaper and plans to establish a City Bureau documenters site for local coverage. The content focuses on Local government reporting; Student-driven media (newspaper, radio, TV); Community media across disciplines; Audio and broadcast news writing.
Grambling state university is starting to work with a local news partner and is part of Lousiana news collective.
The Long Island Advocate publishes student journalism focused on off-campus reporting, with an emphasis on six communities around Hofstra University.

Student journalists research, write, produce and broadcast news for WRHU, an FCC-licensed community radio station owned and operated by Hofstra University. it is a non-credit semester-long club. Local/Community focused reporting on Long Island and New York State. Distribution Radio, Print and Social Media platforms.

Led by senior-level journalism majors under the supervision of professional journalists and professors, HUNS provides hyperlocal and national multimedia reporting to fill news gaps and offer more diverse news coverage.

Program Name: English Associates in Arts Degree program
Structure: The English Associates in Arts degree program  (with a specialization in Journalism and Media Studies) offers a specialized curriculum for a small cohort of approximately 25 students—with 14 students in journalism.

Practicum class with a different investigation project each semester. Course is required for master's students in investigations or data journalism. The school offers tuition offset for graduate students, who work as fellows. For undergraduates, the course offers internships credit for the first year and hourly pay beyond that year. Students do research, writing, photo, video, graphics, databases, depending on the needs of the project. There are also internship credit and paid opportunities for more experienced students

A complex, multi-layered system currently moving toward a "teaching hospital" model to integrate professional public media with student experiential learning to provide deeper community coverage while fulfilling the university's academic mission
.
The structural components of the IU program include:
Integrated Public Media: This division includes a professional TV station and radio station, as well as student media outlets like the Indiana Daily Student (IDS) newspaper, a low-power FM student radio station, and a student TV production group
While content is currently shared on university-hosted platforms like the IDS and Indiana Public Media websites, the program is working to build a formal syndication system
. This would allow student-produced and professionally edited content to be distributed to news organizations across the state

The Mobile and Social Journalism course is course-based, with a strong focus on practical, hands-on reporting. The program encourages students to publish their work on Ithaca Week if it meets professional editorial standards. They have a news-academic partnership with The Rochester Beacon, a nonprofit community online news site.

The work from the courses of this program are published on the college's digital platform, The Volunteer. The main emphasis has been sports, though students have also covered community topics. The stories aren't yet co-published with outside organizations, though the faculty champion has a relationship with an R1 univeristy and intends to partner with their media program.

Cliff Anthony is both the faculty adviser for the student newspaper  and also teaches a journalism course. He uses both as sources for stories that can be pitched to local news partners. The "partnerships" with local media are informal and based on a pitching model. Anthony identifies good story ideas and asks students if they are willing to cover them; once a story is underway, he pitches it to local editors at outlets like cleveland.com, The Land, and the Gazette. External Editing Process: When students write for mainstream outlets, Anthony’s involvement is "much greater" to ensure a "clean copy" for understaffed professional newsrooms. He reviews the work for content "holes," suggests new leads, and fixes AP style errors before submission. Distribution and Output: In addition to digital content, the program produces one print edition per month. The print edition includes links to stories that appeared online to drive traffic to the web.
Students in the NewsLab and in classes produce stories which are shared with media outlets around Ohio, with a particular focus on rural community outlets.
This course is a partnership with Lehigh Valley public media. Students spend the first month learning basic audio production, then go on to produce pieces for the station.

Program Name: LSU Cold Case Project (Agricultural & Mechanical College)
Structure: Louisiana State University’s Cold Case Project is a senior level class within the Manship School of Mass Communication. Through the course, taught by Christopher Drew, students investigate Civil Rights-era cold cases. Students’ work is made available for any news publication to pick up free of charge. Drew notifies outlets about the stories through an email list maintained for distributing LSU’s Statehouse Bureau stories. Students have also produced digital products, like videos and two podcast series, which were distributed through local public radio stations.

Program Name: Manship School Statehouse Bureau (Agricultural & Mechanical College)
Structure:
The Louisiana State University Manship School Statehouse Bureau has been covering the statehouse in Baton Rouge since 2016. The program includes a prerequisite class in the fall, a spring class based at the Capitol, which is just a short drive from campus during the legislative session, and a paid summer program for a handful of students to cover the end of the session once the semester ends. The program is managed by faculty member Christopher Drew, who had a 40-year career as a journalist prior to starting at LSU in 2017. There are typically 10 to 12 students a semester. Students receive 3 credits and can take the class twice. Students are required to spend at least one day in the legislature.

Distribution: 4 publications, digital publications, newspapers, and radio/TV have run at least one of their stories and many take content on a regular basis.

Program Name: Louisiana Collegiate News Collaborative (Agricultural & Mechanical College)
Structure:
The Louisiana Collegiate News Collaborative appears to be the first of its kind in the U.S. Louisiana State University, which is leading the collaborative, is working with seven other higher education institutions in Louisiana to work with faculty and news outlets to produce student-reported community news across the state. Those schools are, Grambling University (HBCU), Dillard University (HBCU), Xavier University (HBCU), Southern University (HBCU), Nicholls State University, Northwestern State University and Southeastern Louisiana University. The collaborative is headed by two faculty at LSU, student media advisor Jeff Gauger and professional-in-residence Lisa Page, who graduated from Dillard University before working at the Times-Picayune and Washington Post. Gauger sends out a weekly email to the network of participating faculty and editors. He is also forming a website for the collaboration. Each school will have initially TO Most of the schools have two to four students who (will get/are) paid $12 an hour for up to 10 hours of work a week during the fall and spring semesters. Additionally, (each school will/most of the schools host one full-time summer internship where the student will be paid $12 an hour to write stories for their local community outlets. To encourage more stories out of classrooms, students reporting for a class can earn a $100 stipend for any publishable story at any of these schools. Individual schools can decide whether students can also earn credit by participating this program.

Distribution: Reporting can include print, digital, broadcast and social media.

The program is part of the university's Department of Mass Communication. Students previously published outside of the program with local outlets, the station has brought in professionals from external outlets. The program is a special topics class for 3 hours of credit per semester. Stories are edited every Monday & Friday by the faculty champion.
Community reporting takes place in a course related to the Greyhound student newspaper and is shared with the Baltimore Fishbowl local news platform.
Students in a documentary production class at Marist University partner with local nonprofit newsrooms to create short video documentaries.
Students produce journalistic work for publication with media partners and the university website.

Students collaborate with the online news publication Bucket List Community Cafe in Denver alongside  CU Boulder and University of Denver students, and all students must produce six articles per semester.

The Great Lakes Echo is a regional environmental news service run by the Knight Center for Environmental Journalism at Michigan State University. An adjunct professor edits the stories, most of which are produced by MSU students. Content is free to use by news outlets in the region at no fee or charge. The Echo covers news on and for the Great Lakes states, particularly the parts of the states that are in the Great Lakes Basin.

The Michigan State University Capital News Service is in its 44th year of covering statehouse news, with an average of five to six students, graduate and undergraduate, per semester in the spring and fall. The four-credit class is capped at 15 students. While students are not required to be at the Capitol every week, the class includes off-campus interviews most Monday mornings with influential players such as the governor, a state agency head, an advocacy group leader or a legislator to kickstart stories that are due at the end of each week. The legislature meets year-round with some breaks and is three miles from campus. Former Michigan Capital reporter Eric Freedman instructs the course.

Distribution: Students provide news coverage to more than 35 news outlets.

A summer special topics course where journalism students go to Bonaroo Music & Arts Festival in Manchester TN. Media arts and live audio students are involved in TV production, while the faculty champion takes journalism students to cover the festival in partnership with the Tennessee Press Association. Stories are written in real-time and sent out to every newspaper in Tennessee to use for free.

Program Name: Sports Reporting with the Bowie Sun
Structure: This program publishes culturally relevant student-reported and edited sports journalism in The Bowie Sun, a digital newspaper covering the racially diverse city of Bowie, Maryland.

Murray state has several new initiatives this fall, including student reporting in a partnership with WKMS, the NPR affiliate on campus and working with a capstone class called 270 Stories to produce local and regional stories for local publications.

Institution Name: New Mexico State University - Department of Journalism and Media Studies
Structure: The program is a 30-minute newscast produced under the NMSU Department of Journalism and Media Studies. The newscast is presented for 25 minutes in English and 3 minutes at the end in Spanish. Two faculty members oversee each branch. Both are presented on KRWG Public Media 3 days a week. The faculty champion is pushing to have stories published in AP style on a digital platform called CrimsonWire, also under the NMSU Department of Journalism and Media Studies. The program is primarily volunteer work, though some students have received credit. The content for the program is edited by a paid student producer and a news director. The faculty champion oversees content produced in Spanish.

The university operates several online media outlets including Pavement Pieces (graduate students) and Cooper Squared (undergraduates). Student reporting completed within specific classes and independently is also regularly published and shared to media outlets ranging from hyper-local sites to major national media organizations.

In this statewide collaborative, eight universities including four HBCUs are partnering together to provide community news through paid student reporting year-round: Louisiana State University and Agricultural & Mechanical College; Grambling University (HBCU), Dillard University (HBCU), Xavier University (HBCU), Southern University (HBCU), Nicholls State University, Northwestern State University and Southeastern University. Each school will have initially two to four students who will get paid $12 an hour for up to 10 hours of work a week during the fall and spring semesters. Additionally, each school will host one full-time summer internship where the student will be paid $12 an hour to write stories for their local community outlets. Reporting can include print, digital, broadcast and social media. To encourage more stories out of classrooms, students reporting for a class can earn a $100 stipend for any publishable story at any of these schools. Individual schools can decide whether students can also earn credit by participating this program.

It is a new emerging program that search publishing student stories with local newspapers such as The New Journal and Guide, partnering with a Black newspaper called New Journal and Guide in Norfolk for publication

Institution Name: North Carolina Agricultural and Technical State University
Structure: Students reporters under the direction of professional editors share content with area media partners and in collaboration with the student newspaper. North Carolina Agricultural and Technical State University Professor David Squires and A&T Register advisor Jennifer Fernandez are the primary editor-supervisors for the local writing project. Most stories first appear on Storieswetell.news. Local media can pick them up from there.

Northeastern's The Scope digital magazine covers news on and for Boston’s core neighborhoods including aspects of civil life such as local elections and some municipal meetings, and content is available and reprinted for free by other hyperlocal news organizations in the Boston area. Over the past four years, the Scope has collaborated with several other hyperlocal news organizations including the Bay State Banner. The program includes 8-10 students, including 4 graduate students, in the partnership, per semester. Most Scope stories focus on social issues or civic affairs, many grown from class projects. Other are assigned by the editor in chief or pitched by paid staffers.
In the Advanced News Media Workshop, a required course, students produce stories for LINK nky, which are published online.

In this statewide collaborative, eight universities including four HBCUs are partnering together to provide community news through paid student reporting year-round: Louisiana State University and Agricultural & Mechanical College; Grambling University (HBCU), Dillard University (HBCU), Xavier University (HBCU), Southern University (HBCU), Nicholls State University, Northwestern State University and Southeastern University. Reporting can include print, digital, broadcast and social media. To encourage more stories out of classrooms, students reporting for a class can earn a $100 stipend for any publishable story at any of these schools. Individual schools can decide whether students can also earn credit by participating this program.
NSU-TV is a student news organization that produces news stories for two local TV stations.

The local news reporting fellowship program sends a team of graduate and undergraduate students to report from Springfield during the legislative session, producing stories in partnership with the nonprofit Capitol News Illinois (CNI), which disseminates content to some 700 print, digital and broadcast outlets around Illinois and bordering states. Medill Senior Lecturer Storer H. (Bob) Rowley, former national editor and foreign correspondent for the Chicago Tribune, directs the student bureau, edits their stories and works with editors at Capitol News Illinois to get the stories published. Students commit to three months of statehouse reporting during the fellowship for one academic quarter after they are chosen; they get stipends, lodging and transportation aid funded by a grant from the Robert R. McCormick Foundation.
In the Medill Investigative Lab, students work on in-depth investigative journalism projects, which are co-published with national and local outlets.

The program is a non-academic unit under the university's Scripps College of Communication where students volunteer their time to gain experiental learning in the program's newsrooms. Students produce 30 minute newscasts weekly, provide coverage on high school and university sports, host a sports talk show on AM radio and on podcast, and a news information podcast. Some students (mainly department heads) have received pay. Students work for $15 for 15 hours a week, a rate set by the university. The program currently employs 6 professional staff who supervise/edit student work across each area.

The program is a community journalism and citizen training program for students and community members. The program recruits students throughout Oklahoma as part of an independent study. These students typically write about their home communities. The training program is an entry-level general education program that enables high school juniors and seniors to take the course for free.
Students produce multimedia reporting for the Pleasantville Press, a hyperlocal news site that covers the community around the university’s Pleasantville campus.
It is a largely student run newspaper (although  a faculty member makes the final editing process) that then other local media republishes.

Students write in-depth stories in partnership with local media organizations, sometimes as part of the “In-Depth” reporting class but the focus is always off-campus serving community news needs, sometimes for credit, pay or as volunteers.

With the technical support of PSU media staff students attend and pull highlights from high school sports games around the region each Friday night, making the highlight reel available to for re-broadcast on six TV stations.
Students attend public meetings and write reports that are then reviewed, edited and circulated to stakeholders via text, email and a website for their community and surrounding counties.

CCR is a thirty-minute news show produced live twice a week focused on the greater community and coordinated through a “capstone” like class and re-broadcast on the student TV station, WPSU and streamed on five other state broadcast partners in surrounding counties.

Program Name: Penn State Greater Allegheny-Tube City Collaborative News Service
Structure: Students in any degree can take newspaper practicum courses and business writing courses where news writing and public relations writing are included in the curriculum.  The  Penn State Greater Allegheny campus student newspaper, The GA-Zette, and Tube City Community Media, LLC, that consists of Tube City Almanac (digital), Tube City Online Radio, and "2 Rivers, 30 Minutes," share news across the partnership. Throughout the process, students will be supervised by the faculty and the Tube City staff, when stories are generated first by the media.
The Point Park News Service publishes student-produced content online, and the university works with them to get the content republished in local, professional outlets.

Program Name: Pittsburgh Media Partnership - Teaching Newsroom (previously mislabled)
Structure: Recent graduates, current students and interns at different Pittsburgh universities fill gaps in local reporting coordinated by a full-time staff editor.

Program Name: New Jersey State House News Service
Structure: The New Jersey Statehouse News Service is a collaborative nonprofit involving Rowan University, The College of New Jersey, and Rutgers University, with potential future involvement from other universities.  The service currently partners with 22 organizations comprising more than 100 news outlets. The number of hours students work for the program varies by school. They’re all asked to write one to two weekly stories: a straight news story and a more ambitious enterprise story.

The Queen’s University student reporting program operates through the ‘Student Media Practicum’ course and the Queens University News Service to provide content to 12 local community news organizations. The program focuses on underreported beats (religion, art, non-profits, environment, behind-the-scenes sports). The program also leverages cross-disciplinary collaboration with faculty in political science, sociology, biology, and other fields, enhancing content quality and student learning.

The program is a hub for research and media production/journalism where students and faculty engage in diverse projects tied to social justice issues. The program has developed a partnership with a local community news outlet (El Comercial News) for internships, and is trying to create more similar partnerships for publishing.  The program encompasses a number of smaller projects, such as Migrant Voices, Writing Roots prodcast, and the student newspaper. Students in this program work as interns or for course credit. Editors are either students or a faculty depending on the project.
The program has external partnerships where journalism and environmental science students produce climate and environmental journalism, particularly focused on South Jersey issues.

Program Name: New Jersey State House News Service
Structure: The New Jersey Statehouse News Service is a collaborative nonprofit involving Rowan University, The College of New Jersey, and Rutgers University, with potential future involvement from other universities.  The service currently partners with 22 organizations comprising more than 100 news outlets. The number of hours students work for the program varies by school. They’re all asked to write one to two weekly stories: a straight news story and a more ambitious enterprise story.

Program Name: New Jersey State House News Service
Structure: The New Jersey Statehouse News Service is a collaborative nonprofit involving Rowan University, The College of New Jersey, and Rutgers University, with potential future involvement from other universities.  The service currently partners with 22 organizations comprising more than 100 news outlets. The number of hours students work for the program varies by school. They’re all asked to write one to two weekly stories: a straight news story and a more ambitious enterprise story.
This program is a classical news partnership media with community news outlets in the region that publish students news.

Lead Faculty Name: James Castonguay (does not teach class) Anne Marie Soma is the professor
Structure: Faculty and students at Scared Heart produce stories for the Easton Courier. Anne Marie Soma teaches a course that is directly associated with the publication. Easton Courier is the local outlet, and is the sole local news partner.
Students produce multimedia stories through partnerships with KUT (Austin's NPR affiliate) KXAN (Austin's NBC affiliate).
WSCL is comprised of 3 different stations under the umbrella of 2 state universities. Students receive credit but are not paid. Students work with professional editors to gain experience.

Program Name: Utah College Media Collaborative, Amplify Utah
Structure: They have both courses and internship programs. Mainly courses integrate real-world journalism projects in collaboration with professional media outlets. They also have strong partnerships with Amplify Utah, The Salt Lake Tribune, KRCL, The Other Side Academy, PBS Utah, and The Great Salt Lake Collaborative. Their content focuses on Community storytelling, underrepresented voices, homelessness, media literacy, and local news coverage.
Student reported local stories are reviewed and edited and made available on a selective basis to local media partners and in collaboration with the student paper, the Houstonian.

The program is a result of a partnership between the university's School of Journalisma and Media Studies and the Institute of Public Strategy's (IPS) Bilingual Reporting Lab. The program produces brief "information goods" which are distributed through La Vocera's WhatsApp channel. During the spring, students in the class develop the information goods into news features, which are then published on La Vocera's website hosted by the university. The faculty champion is the only editor in the program.

Santa Ana College’s student reporting program operates through el Don, a credit-based, faculty-edited newsroom embedded across multiple journalism and media studies courses, where students are required to serve as newsroom staff and publish content as a condition of receiving course credit. The core course, JAMS 123A (Multimedia Production), emphasizes cross-platform reporting, requiring students to produce and adapt stories for web, social media, and multimedia formats, while advanced courses (123B–D) increase publishing expectations and introduce student leadership roles such as Editor-in-Chief, Managing Editor, and Copy Editor. The program engages approximately 30–40 students annually and produces around 300 stories per year, all of which include a required photography component, reflecting a strong emphasis on multimedia storytelling. Students manage day-to-day newsroom operations, but all content is reviewed and edited by faculty prior to publication to ensure professional standards. The program also maintains an informal partnership with Voice of OC, which publishes a small number of student stories annually, serving as a limited external distribution channel. AI use is minimal and pedagogically framed through a “human sandwich” model, where it may assist with brainstorming or refinement but is not used to generate journalistic content, and a formal AI policy is currently under development. Overall, the program emphasizes local reporting, multimedia literacy, and hands-on, in-person journalism training within a structured, curriculum-integrated newsroom model.

The program is a co-curricular unit within the College of Human Development, Culture, and Media. Stories are published in the university's student media outlets and through the program's partners (Fox, NBC, ESPN, Hoops HQ). The faculty champion directs the program and edits all stories.

Senior journalism capstone students work with Investigate Midwest on public affairs journalism
projects that are eligible of publication at the site. Faculty teach the course and assist students."

The Arrow student news organization operates as a complex news-academic partnership between the university and Rust Communications, the parent company of the local newspaper. The program is overseen by a leadership team composed of four professionals from the partner organization, the Arrow’s faculty advisor, and the department chairperson, who acts as the budget custodian. While the partnership follows a publisher agreement model, editorial decisions are made entirely by a paid staff of 10 to 12 student workers

The media programs at the university fall under student affairs. The faculty champion is working to integrate their communications department and student affairs to work with students on submitting stories either to the student newspaper, The Lion's Roar, or to local media. The faculty champion also has a number of connections to local outlets and has helped students get their work published in the past.

In this statewide collaborative, eight universities including four HBCUs are partnering together to provide community news through paid student reporting year-round: Louisiana State University and Agricultural & Mechanical College; Grambling University (HBCU), Dillard University (HBCU), Xavier University (HBCU), Southern University (HBCU), Nicholls State University, Northwestern State University and Southeastern University. Each school will have initially two to four students who will get paid $12 an hour for up to 10 hours of work a week during the fall and spring semesters. Additionally, each school will host one full-time summer internship where the student will be paid $12 an hour to write stories for their local community outlets. Reporting can include print, digital, broadcast and social media. To encourage more stories out of classrooms, students reporting for a class can earn a $100 stipend for any publishable story at any of these schools. Individual schools can decide whether students can also earn credit by participating this program.
This program has a news-academic partnership with the Chattanooga Times Free Press, publishing student-reported community news stories.

Faculty leaders incorporate a project every semester into classes that are shared on the CT Collaborative news site for news outlets in the state. 

"It's more about integrating a project or identifying students who are working on a statewide story and connecting them to the collaborative." - Jodie

Molly and Julia Rendelman co-direct the lab. They work with group of students who take on stories with the goal of getting it published in either the student paper, or other local news outlets. The lab also has a strong relationship with the student paper, the Daily Egyptian. Capital News Illinois is the main local news outlet partner. Lab is not course-based, but there are instances in which students from Molly's classes contribute stories.

Institution Name: Southern University and Agricultural & Mechanical College
Structure: Louisiana State University, which is leading the collaborative, is working with seven other higher education institutions in Louisiana to work with faculty and news outlets to produce student-reported community news across the state. Those schools are: Grambling University (HBCU), Dillard University (HBCU), Xavier University (HBCU), Southern University (HBCU), Nicholls State University, Northwestern State University and Southeastern University. The collaborative will be headed by two faculty at LSU: student media advisor Jeff Gauger and professional-in-residence Lisa Page, who graduated from Dillard University before working at the Times-Picayune and Washington Post.

Program Name: Utah College Media Collaborative, Amplify Utah - audio practicum class is what Ryan actually teaches.
Structure: Audio practicum class that is centered around our campus radio station KSUU FM, and in spring 2025, three students from Ryan's practicum class  worked on projects for the regional collaborative. It was a 5-episode podcast seires

The faculty leader works with the station to find opportunities for students to help with live broadcast for high school sports coverage. Students will often provide the color analysis, help broadcast the game or provide the commentary for the live stream.
Students at St. Bonaventure University’s Jandoli School of Communication report for the online news site TAPinto Greater Olean as part of an experiential learning news coverage course.
Students in a news reporting class produce stories for local news outlet the Winooski News under the direction and editing of the faculty leader.

This program includes a practicum class wherein students write and publish stories for Stories of Atlantic City, a collaborative digital news project that aims to supplement meaningful reporting in the area.

Working Newsroom is a journalism course that produces student content for local media partners. They accept assignments from our news partners rather than have them pick up what they want from a website.
Student reporters with the Canal Side Chronicles website are creating new partnerships to include local community papers for publishing content.
SUNY Broome Community College is working with the Institute for Local News at SUNY to support local media partnerships.
SUNY Cortland students report and write local news articles that are published by local media partners under a partnership launched in 2025.  Its acourse based program.

For more than 45 years SUNY New Paltz has been running the Legislative Gazette, an online media outlet that reports on the New York Legislature. The program is funded through the University as well as grants from a number of organizations, including the Knight Foundation and the Ottaway Foundation. Each semester 10 to 15 students make the 70- mile-one-way commute to Albany from New Paltz. Editor and New Paltz professor James Gormley leads the class and students have the option to take between one and twelve credits during the semester.
The university is developing plans for programs that would involve students reporting for local media organizations and expanding campus media off-campus reporting.
Students report on community news for local media partner Iron String Press, publisher of local news website AllOtsego.com and newspapers including Hometown Oneonta.
Students in an advanced audio production class cover local government, events, arts and entertainment news and other topics for WSKG, a local NPR member station.
Student reporters in classes report for local community papers and produce audio for radio station re-broadcast.
Supervised experiences in professional workplace settings for qualified upper-level Communication Studies, Broadcast Journalism and Media production majors.
In the Journalism Program, community reporting is done in conjunction with our city's daily newspaper, the Press-Republican.
Classes (credit-based), faculty review stories before submitting to partners.
SUNY Polytechnic Institute will launch an audio storytelling course in fall 2025 with a focus on producing audio news content for local media partners.

Student reporters analyze and organize documents obtained through public records requests related to police misconduct in New York and report on their findings in stories published by local media partners. Its a  Course w/internship opportunity for select students. Distribution is on Print Primarilly with video pieces.

Students volunteer and intern at NPR member station WAER (a Syracuse University licensee), working in areas including sports coverage, news reporting, podcast and audio editing, web writing, on-air hosting and social media content creation.

Student media production is anchored by Newshouse.com, a long-running, school-supported digital platform that functions both as a curricular publishing outlet and as an open contribution space for student reporters, editors, photographers, videographers, and data journalists. Content is produced through multiple classes as well as volunteer participation and is publicly distributed through Newshouse, with selective cross-publication through affiliated and external partners including WAER. In addition, the school operates a year-long, topic-driven reporting project open to students of any major, focused on issues of public relevance such as immigration, misinformation, or Title IX equity, with work published on a project-specific site and distributed through national and regional outlets including USA. Today, Syracuse.com, and Indian Country Today.
Texan News Service stories are written by students in journalism and broadcasting classes and can be reprinted or reposted at no cost to partnering news outlets.
Students in a capstone reporting class work with community newspapers to report stories. Students are assigned beats at their papers and pursue these stories through the spring semester.
Students in a summer course cover the community of Hurst, Texas, and publish their stories to a website and collaborate with a local media outlet.

Program Name: The Logan Center for Urban Investigative Reporting
Structure: The Logan Center for Urban Investigative Reporting is a program based at Temple University’s Klein College of Media and Communication that focuses on community-based urban investigative reporting. Directed by Yvonne Latty, the center operates through a model of academic-media partnerships, collaborating with local news organizations to produce high-impact journalism. The center’s primary goal is to provide students with professional investigative experience while serving the Philadelphia community.

Lead Faculty Name: Arthur Murray (Maybe, we are not sure)
Structure: Students produce stories for KTSU2 that are shared with local media partners.
In the course, students work on health data journalism projects that are published in local outets.

Institution Name: The University of Texas at El Paso
Structure: The program is both course-based and internship-based. It includes COMM 4313 (Senior Capstone), COMM 2344 (Digital Audio and Video Production), and COMM 3313 (News Gathering and Investigation), all of which provide hands-on journalism training. The program has strong partnerships with professional news outlets, allowing student work to be republished and amplified. There is also growing collaboration with other departments, such as Digital Media Production, Public Relations, and Media Advertising, to expand multimedia storytelling and revenue-generating projects.
Students in an introductory journalism course produce stories that are published on two local Reno news sites; ThisisReno.com and the Reno News and Review.
Journalism students cover a range of local stories for local newspaper Spotlight News.

The University of Alabama owns three public-facing news outlets, which operate out of the university’s Digital Media Center: the television station WVUA 23, Alabama Public Radio, and the Center for Public Television. The media outlets each function as independent, standalone organizations, though they work in conjunction with the Department of Journalism and Creative Media, offering multiple opportunities for students to contribute to local news as interns or volunteers. Different opportunities may be paid or for course credit.  Through these opportunities, students participate in the newsroom, interacting with full-time professional journalistic staff, attending daily news meetings, and pitching and producing stories. Classes within the Department of Journalism and Creative Media may also interact with the news outlets, including by using lab space in the Digital Media Center. In one graduate-level class, students have opportunities to develop and pitch stories for Digital Media Center outlets. The Digital Media Center offers opportunities for students from disciplines across the university, not only students in the Department of Journalism and Creative Media.

The University of Alabama owns three public-facing news outlets, which operate out of the university’s Digital Media Center: the television station WVUA 23, Alabama Public Radio, and the Center for Public Television. The media outlets each function as independent, standalone organizations, though they work in conjunction with the Department of Journalism and Creative Media, offering multiple opportunities for students to contribute to local news as interns or volunteers. Different opportunities may be paid or for course credit.  Through these opportunities, students participate in the newsroom, interacting with full-time professional journalistic staff, attending daily news meetings, and pitching and producing stories. Classes within the Department of Journalism and Creative Media may also interact with the news outlets, including by using lab space in the Digital Media Center. In one graduate-level class, students have opportunities to develop and pitch stories for Digital Media Center outlets. The Digital Media Center offers opportunities for students from disciplines across the university, not only students in the Department of Journalism and Creative Media.

WVUA represents an atypical hybrid model in which a university-owned, commercial television station simultaneously serves the general public across seven counties in West Alabama and functions as an intensive student training environment. Unlike student publications oriented toward campus audiences, WVUA produces daily local newscasts for community viewers, sells advertising, and operates with a small professional staff overseeing a predominantly student workforce. Approximately 70–100 students participate annually across newsroom, sports, and production roles, contributing to an estimated 7,000 stories, with students responsible for nearly all reporting and much of the anchoring. Students must either receive academic credit or paid internship compensation, with strict volunteering largely eliminated, and typically progress from writing and editing roles into on-air reporting as they gain experience. Editorial review is conducted primarily by professional staff, though student producers may edit copy during evenings and weekends.

The program exists under the science and environmental journalism major. Students in a course co-publish their stories with the Fairbanks Daily News-Miner. Students in the program are required to take an internship, in the past students have taken the internship with the public radio station as well. Stories are edited by the faculty champion.

Arizona Sonoran News currently is an upper-level class, and one of the options students have to do professional practice. Kendal teaches the course with an adjunct who is a full-time journalist. Students find stories, pitch them, reporting them and then produce them. A goal is to also translate as many stories as possible in Spanish. Professors edit and send weekly/bi-weekly email newsletters pitching stories to outlets. This version of AZSN started 3 years ago. In some limited cases, students from other classes have also contributed to the newswire.

The program is an off-shoot of a cohort called Community Journalism. The faculty champion directs the cohort and teaches the newswriting class. The other part of the cohort is a class where students conduct photojournalism. The faculty champion is responsible for vetting, selecting, and sending the stories produced in the cohort to the media partner (KUAF, public radio station licensed by the University of Arkansas). Students receive credit for both courses.

Program Name: Advanced Radio Reporting/ KUAF Public Radio
Structure: The faculty champion is the senior producer for a local news magazine show for an NPR station in NW Arkansas. The faculty champion conducts reporting and producing and co-hosting for a show called Ozarks At Large. In the spring of 2023, the faculty champion started as an adjunct instructor at the University of Arkansas teaching a radio reporting class. The students work with the faculty champion, gaining experience in producing stories being in a newsroom. The course is for credit and stories are edited by the faculty champion.

The university launched a Community Reporting course in fall 2025 designed to function like a working newsroom; students will work with local media partners to report on community issues in the greater Buffalo region.

UC Berkeley Graduate School of Journalism has two community news sites, Richmond Confidential and Oakland North, both of which are housed within the School’s Investigative Reporting Program. These news sites have a total of five classes devoted to them, three for Oakland North and two for Richmond Confidential. About 60 graduate students write for these two sites in the fall via those classes.Spring semester offers a Local News Enterprise class, whereby about a dozen students work on longer form projects, about one story per month. These stories are not always publishable as they have to reach a certain bar. In the summer, the program has two paid interns, one per news site and who each publish stories at least twice weekly. In addition to Berkeley’s two news sites, students also produce stories for local news organizations. The program works with two consistent daily news sites where student work gets published in: The Oaklandside and Richmondside, both community newsrooms launched by a Bay Area nonprofit called CitySide. The program is part of several external collaborative initiatives, including the California Local News Fellowship and the California Reporting Project, which tasked students to investigate police excessive force records and cases across the state in collaboration with local newsrooms. Major investigative projects like this are in outlets like the Dallas Morning News, LA Times, Grist, and the San Francisco Chronicle.

It's a course-based reporting program. In classes, students' work is edited and shared with the Orlando Sentinel and the local PBS and NPR affiliates. Through class assignments, students cover community issues that help augment the local coverage of partner outlets.

The program will draw student-produced content from 3 different courses in the university: a capstone, Digital Media: Student Experience, an english department course, Journalism, and an interdisciplinary/internship course. The editor will depend on the course they're taking.

Capstone class that is encouraged for and prefers seniors who are near the end of their college experience. Students develop in-depth stories over the semester and report on them, either individually or in teams. News partners are involved in pitch process, and are sent links afterward. In the past, has worked with Colorado Sun as well as smaller publications in Denver, such as the Bucket List. Regular partners also include Colorado Public Radio and Denver Gazette.

Program Name: Connecticut Student Journalism Collaborative, News Writing 2 class
Structure: Faculty teach a course that is the second in a two-part series of in-depth reporting classes, the second of which teaches students how to do community reporting and identify high quality works and offer to the regional collaboration. In the past, Julie has also assigned story to a student who was not in the news writing class, but that story was also published by the collaboration, and republished by local news outlets. The collaborative also has professional editors.

The University of Delaware in the spring of 2026 will launch a six-credit fellowship from January to June. Students will get one credit in January, three credits during the school semester, and two more credits for continuing into June. The fellowship will be offered for four to two students and is open to students majoring or minoring in public policy, political science, political communications, journalism, or other communications. The fellowship will fulfill a capstone degree requirement for any arts and sciences student participating at the 400 level. It also fulfills electives for the journalism minor. Nancy Karibjanian, Director of the Journalism Program at the University of Delaware who will be running the program, hopes to limit the fellowship to juniors to avoid making a senior commit to the June portion of the program. UD’s campus in Newark is about 50 minutes away by car from the state capitol in Dover. Every student will also be asked to follow one bill, with Karibjanian’s approval for newsworthiness. 
Distribution: The program will mix broadcast and digital print reporting. The outlet’s editor will edit the students’ work, and Karibjanian plans to use a Listserv to distribute stories to partnering organizations. Students can take assignments from local radio or news outlets at any point during the semester. Karibjanian will review any assignments from the outlets to approve the story concept for its legitimacy and relevance for a student journalist.
Looking forward: It’s unclear if transportation can be arranged or if students will have to drive themselves. Karibjanian said students may have to start by listening to the live stream of floor debates as a backup, in case they can’t transport themselves to the statehouse in person. 
Notable: Karibjanian wants the students to cover stories other outlets are not covering; she is still working out partnerships with local outlets beyond Delaware Public Media and is exploring partnerships with other local radio and broadcast stations.
A required capstone course where students make short-form documentaries and an 800-word digital story that are shared with Rocky Mountain PBS, which airs some of the final films.

The University of Florida’s Fresh Take Florida runs two semesters per year and employs up to 18 students per semester to cover the Florida Legislature, which is 2.5 hours from their campus in Gainesville. The three-credit class started in 2019 and operates during both the spring and fall semesters. While students are not usually paid, the program covers travel, dorm stays at Florida State University’s campus in Tallahassee and access to public records thanks in part to donations via the University of Florida Foundation. While many students will choose to take the class for two semesters, single-semester students will typically opt to take the class in the spring because it coincides with the legislative session. Most students come from within the journalism program. Students must write and publish five stories for the semester, and some have gone beyond that number. One of the five stories has to focus on an underrepresented community

Noticias WUFT, a Spanish-language news operation housed within public media, is dedicated to informing, educating, and connecting the Hispanic community in North Central Florida through accurate, culturally relevant, and community-focused journalism. As part of this initiative at the University of Florida, students enrolled in journalism and communication courses produce content across television, radio, and digital platforms.
The program established a bilingual student newsroom that not only serves community information needs but also provides professional training, with many graduates advancing into major media markets. Operating under an immersion-for-academic-credit model within the UFCJC Division of Media Properties, student work is closely supervised and edited by faculty. This model has since been adopted more broadly across the College, enabling students to integrate experiential learning directly into their coursework.
Independent public radio station WUFT is staffed by student reporters, editors and producers.

Grady Newsource is a broadcast and digital-first news service via a journalism capstone class serving five counties in Northeast Georgia, and has partnerships with local news outlets to pick up and republish stories.

The Oglethorpe Echo is a weekly newspaper currently operating as a nonprofit, and editorially staffed by student reporters in a capstone class managed by a full-time professional journalist who servces as the paper's editor.

For one weekend every spring, 18-20 photojournalism students descend on a rural county in northern Georgia, reporting stories about the communities and people who live there. First launched in 2006 by lecturer Mark Johnson, the workshop is the signature event of the Grady College of Journalism and Mass Communication’s documentary photojournalism course each spring. (Student enrolled in the fall semester get a similar experience during a workshop at the state fair.) Over the last 20 years, the workshop has been held in 17 different counties. Before the workshop begins, students develop and pitch story ideas, so when they arrive in the county for that weekend, they’re ready to jump into their reporting. Johnson invites experienced photojournalists to come work through the weekend as coaches, reviewing the material students gather with them each day. Before each workshop, Johnson establishes partnerships with local news outlets to distribute the stories. After each workshop, local news partners often publish initial photos spreads. Then, the outlets have the option to pick up students’ projects, including photo essays and multimedia pieces. The student work is also published on the college’s website.
 
The class includes a mixture of students in their second and third photojournalism semesters. This mixture creates a dynamic where the more experienced students serve as mentors for students who are not as far along. “Part of the program's core belief is that knowledge is not yours, and so you have a responsibility to help others within the class,” says Johnson. “The goal is for everybody to get better at this.”

The University of Hawaiʻi at Mānoaʻs journalism program began collaborating specifically on state legislative coverage in Spring 2022 and chose as its partner an innovative online-only news organization called Civil Beat. Students focus on reporting on the spring session of the Hawaii State Legislature in Honolulu. Each student selects a beat at the beginning of the semester and is responsible for coming up with story ideas connecting that beat with current legislative action. In the first year, the program had 12 students participate; in 2023, they had 15 students; in 2024, another 22 students were involved.
Student reported multi-media stories are shared from a capstone class  in partnership with local media as part of a new news bureau.

Program Name: McClure Journalism Internship Program/State Capitol News Bureau
Structure: The University of Idaho’s Journalism and Mass Media McClure Journalism Internship Program hosts about two students per spring semester to cover the Idaho Legislature in Boise, which is 300 miles away from the university campus in Moscow. During the week, students also get additional media training through the School’s news service, Idaho Public Radio. Students spend about every weekday and sometimes weekends, depending on whether there’s a news event, during January, February and March working at the statehouse during the session. Students tend to be juniors and seniors.

Since 1972, the University of Illinois Springfield has offered a Master’s Degree in Public Affairs Reporting. The 10-month program allows students to have hands-on experience with political reporting. The program is funded through the university, along with the help of private donors and alumni. With the funding structure of the program in addition to stipends and scholarships offered to students, Illinois residents are able to complete the program for around $6,000. The program is run by Jason Piscia, a longtime newspaper editor and reporter, and a graduate of the program. In a typical year, Piscia teaches and oversees around 10 students.

Stories are published on the Illinois Public Media sites, where their established viewership can read them. Most of the stories are pitched by students themselves and then edited by either Evans or one of the paid deputy producers – students who are more experienced and can help guide their peers. In 2020 the student newsroom produced more than 170 student pieces, and that number doubled the following year resulting in about 350 student stories in the 2021-22 school year. There are two classes of students that are involved with the program and three students are hired each semester to work as deputy producers. The program is funded by the University.

Students report stories for two community papers, The Mount Vernon-Lisbon Sun and the Solon Economist under the direction of editors and faculty in a partnership between the student paper, The Daily Iowan,  and the School of Journalism and Mass Communication.

Program Name: Good Morning Indian Country Radio News Station
Structure: Good Morning Indian Country is a student-led native news and information program. Students produce 30-minute shows that are formatted like a traditional local television broadcast.
